parts.homeLangDetails: As a verb prefix, 'nach-' often conveys following behind or going deeper into something — here, mentally pursuing a thought after it arises.

words.translationLong: to think about, to reflect on, to ponder, to contemplate
words.exampleSentence: Ich muss kurz nachdenken, bevor ich antworte.
words.exampleTranslation: I need to think for a moment before I answer.

parts.homeLangDetails: A prefix with several senses. In 'versprechen', it converts 'speak' into a formal, binding act — something spoken so as to be held to. Contrast: 'sich versprechen' (to misspeak) — the 'going astray' sense of the same prefix.

words.translationLong: to promise, to vow
words.exampleSentence: Er hat mir versprochen, pünktlich zu sein.
words.exampleTranslation: He promised me he would be on time.

parts.homeLangDetails: From 'treiben' (to drive, to propel). The same root appears in 'Antrieb' (drive/motivation) and 'austreiben' (to expel). Here: to drive something past its proper limit.

words.translationLong: to exaggerate, to overdo, to go too far
words.exampleSentence: Du übertreibst mal wieder total!
words.exampleTranslation: You're totally exaggerating again!

parts.homeLangDetails: From 'stützen' (to support, to prop). Related to 'Stütze' (a support, a pillar). Literally: to place a prop under something.

words.translationLong: to support, to assist, to back
words.exampleSentence: Die Organisation unterstützt Kinder in schwierigen Situationen.
words.exampleTranslation: The organization supports children in difficult situations.

parts.homeLangDetails: A prefix meaning separation or the beginning of a process — often 'un-' or 'de-'. Here it suggests unfolding or unwinding something that was previously wrapped up.
From 'wickeln' (to wrap, to wind). Still used literally: 'ein Baby wickeln' means to wrap/swaddle a baby. 'Entwickeln' is literally to un-wrap — to unfurl potential.

words.translationLong: to develop, to evolve, to create
words.exampleSentence: Das Team hat eine neue App entwickelt.
words.exampleTranslation: The team developed a new app.

parts.homeLangDetails: As a separable prefix, 'auf-' often implies opening up or relinquishing — here, releasing something upward and away from oneself.

words.translationLong: to give up, to quit, to surrender; also: to mail (a letter), to place (an order)
words.exampleSentence: Sie hat das Rauchen endlich aufgegeben.
words.exampleTranslation: She finally gave up smoking.

parts.homeLangDetails: A prefix meaning 'before' or 'in advance'. Here it places the act of readying something ahead of a future event.

words.translationLong: to prepare, to get ready
words.exampleSentence: Wir müssen uns gut auf die Prüfung vorbereiten.
words.exampleTranslation: We need to prepare well for the exam.

parts.homeLangDetails: From 'setzen' (to set, to place). To translate is to 'set across' — carrying meaning from one language to another, as if crossing a river.

words.translationLong: to translate; also (separable): to ferry across
words.exampleSentence: Kannst du diesen Brief ins Englische übersetzen?
words.exampleTranslation: Can you translate this letter into English?

parts.homeLangDetails: A prefix conveying the completion or achievement of a state. In 'erkennen', it transforms 'kennen' (to know/be familiar with) into the act of actively recognizing — bringing recognition into being.

words.translationLong: to recognize, to identify, to realize, to perceive
words.exampleSentence: Ich habe ihn sofort an seiner Stimme erkannt.
words.exampleTranslation: I recognized him immediately by his voice.

parts.homeLangDetails: A separable prefix often implying completion or clearing. 'Auf-' here suggests clearing things upward and away — tidying into order.
From 'Raum' (space, room) + the verbal suffix. 'Räumen' means to clear or vacate a space. The same root appears in 'Wohnraum' (living space) and 'einräumen' (to put away/concede).

words.translationLong: to tidy up, to clean up, to put things away
words.exampleSentence: Bitte räum dein Zimmer auf, bevor du rausgehst.
words.exampleTranslation: Please tidy up your room before you go out.

parts.homeLangDetails: As a separable prefix, 'vor-' places something forward or before. In 'vorstellen', it evokes placing something in front of the mind's eye — or placing oneself in front of another to be seen.

words.translationLong: to imagine, to picture; to introduce oneself
words.exampleSentence: Können Sie sich vorstellen, wie das war?
words.exampleTranslation: Can you imagine what that was like?

parts.homeLangDetails: A prefix of separation or decisive departure. In 'entscheiden', it suggests cutting away alternatives — separating from uncertainty.
From 'scheiden' (to separate, to part). Related to 'Scheide' (sheath, divide) and 'Abschied' (farewell — a parting). The idea: to make a clean cut between options.

words.translationLong: to decide, to make a decision, to choose
words.exampleSentence: Wir müssen uns bis morgen entscheiden.
words.exampleTranslation: We need to make a decision by tomorrow.

parts.homeLangDetails: A prefix that makes intransitive verbs transitive or directs action toward an object. Here it focuses 'werben' (to advertise, to court) onto a position or role — to campaign for oneself.
From 'werben' (to advertise, to recruit, to court). Related to 'Werbung' (advertising). To apply is to actively court or pitch for a position.

words.translationLong: to apply (for a job, a place), to put oneself forward
words.exampleSentence: Sie hat sich um die Stelle als Ingenieurin beworben.
words.exampleTranslation: She applied for the position as an engineer.

parts.homeLangDetails: Here 'ver-' intensifies or enacts the finality of the action — bringing a parting to completion.

'Abschied' itself comes from 'ab-' (away) + 'scheiden' (to separate/part). To 'verabschieden' someone is to formally enact that parting. Used also for passing laws: the parliament 'verabschiedet' a bill — sending it on its way.

words.translationLong: to say goodbye to, to bid farewell; also: to pass (a law)
words.exampleSentence: Wir haben uns am Bahnhof voneinander verabschiedet.
words.exampleTranslation: We said goodbye to each other at the train station.

parts.homeLangDetails: Here 'ver-' carries its 'going astray' sense — the attempted improvement goes wrong.

words.translationShort: to make things worse while trying to improve them
words.translationLong: to worsen something through misguided attempts at improvement
words.exampleSentence: Er hat versucht, den Kratzer auszubessern, aber dabei nur alles verschlimmbessert.
words.exampleTranslation: He tried to fix the scratch but only made everything worse in the process.

parts.homeLangDetails: From 'Weh' (pain, ache) — the same root as in 'Heimweh' (homesickness, literally 'home-ache'). 'Fernweh' is its antonym: an aching pull toward distant places rather than toward home.

words.translationLong: a longing for distant places, the ache of wanderlust
words.exampleSentence: Das Fernweh packt mich jeden Frühling aufs Neue.
words.exampleTranslation: The urge to travel grips me anew every spring.

words.translationLong: pleasure derived from another's misfortune
words.exampleSentence: Es war pure Schadenfreude, als er sah, wie sein Rivale stolperte.
words.exampleTranslation: It was pure schadenfreude when he saw his rival stumble.

parts.homeLangDetails: The suffix '-bar' is equivalent to English '-able' or '-ful' — indicating capacity or quality. 'Dankbar' means thankful/grateful (capable of giving thanks). See also: 'essbar' (edible), 'tragbar' (wearable).
The suffix '-keit' (like '-heit') forms abstract nouns from adjectives. 'Dankbarkeit' is the state or quality of being thankful.

words.translationLong: gratitude, thankfulness, gratefulness
words.exampleSentence: Sie empfand tiefe Dankbarkeit für die Hilfe ihrer Freunde.
words.exampleTranslation: She felt deep gratitude for her friends' help.

parts.homeLangDetails: From 'sich sehnen' (to yearn, to long for). The verb may itself relate to an older sense of 'sinnen' (to sense/to crave) blended with physical aching. The longing here is bodily as much as emotional.

From 'Sucht' (addiction, compulsive craving) — related to 'suchen' (to seek). The same element appears in 'Eifersucht' (jealousy) and 'Gelbsucht' (jaundice). 'Sehnsucht' is a yearning so deep it feels compulsive.

words.translationLong: deep longing, intense yearning, wistful desire
words.exampleSentence: Er empfand eine tiefe Sehnsucht nach seiner alten Heimat.
words.exampleTranslation: He felt a deep longing for his old homeland.

parts.homeLangDetails: 'Gemüt' refers to the inner emotional temperament or disposition — the warmth of a person's soul. Related to 'Mut' (courage, spirit) and 'gemütlich' (cozy, warm, pleasant).
An adjectival suffix equivalent to English '-ly' or '-like'. 'Gemütlich' means warm, cozy, convivial.
Converts the adjective 'gemütlich' into an abstract noun — the quality or atmosphere of being gemütlich.

words.translationLong: a warm, cozy, convivial atmosphere; the quality of comfortable well-being
words.exampleSentence: Das kleine Café verströmte eine echte Gemütlichkeit.
words.exampleTranslation: The little café exuded a genuine sense of coziness.

parts.homeLangDetails: 'Wandern' in German means specifically to hike or roam on foot — not just to wander aimlessly. It has a wholesome, purposeful sense of walking through nature or countryside.
'Lust' in German means desire, urge, or pleasure — broader than the English 'lust'. 'Ich habe Lust' simply means 'I feel like (doing something)'. Here: the pleasurable urge to hike and roam.

words.translationLong: love of hiking and roaming; the urge to travel and explore
words.exampleSentence: Die Wanderlust trieb sie jedes Wochenende in die Berge.
words.exampleTranslation: The love of hiking drove her into the mountains every weekend.

words.translationLong: world-weariness; anguish at the gap between the world as it is and as it should be
words.exampleSentence: Nach den Nachrichten überfiel ihn wieder dieser typische Weltschmerz.
words.exampleTranslation: After the news, that typical sense of world-weariness washed over him again.

parts.homeLangDetails: From 'Gefühl' (feeling, emotion) — itself from 'fühlen' (to feel). 'Mitgefühl' is feeling-with: sharing in another's emotional experience.

words.translationLong: compassion, empathy, sympathy
words.exampleSentence: Sie zeigte großes Mitgefühl für die Betroffenen.
words.exampleTranslation: She showed great compassion for those affected.

parts.homeLangDetails: From 'schließen' (to close, to shut). 'Torschluss' literally means the closing of the gate — historically the moment city gates shut for the night, after which entry was impossible.

words.translationLong: fear that time is running out; panic at a closing window of opportunity
words.exampleSentence: Mit dreißig begann sie, eine leichte Torschlusspanik wegen Kindern zu spüren.
words.exampleTranslation: At thirty, she began to feel a mild panic about running out of time to have children.

parts.homeLangDetails: 'Gleich' means equal, same, or level. In 'Gleichmut', it suggests an even, unvarying inner state — a spirit that stays the same regardless of circumstance.

'Mut' means courage or spirit — the inner disposition of a person. Related to 'Gemüt' (temperament) and 'mutig' (brave). Here it refers to the quality of one's inner bearing: an equable, steady spirit.

words.translationLong: equanimity, composure, even-temperedness
words.exampleSentence: Sie begegnete den schlechten Nachrichten mit bewundernswertem Gleichmut.
words.exampleTranslation: She faced the bad news with admirable equanimity.
